title: British Royal Train
memoir: If anyone is known for traveling in style, it's the U.K.'s Royal Family. Their most iconic mode of transport? The Royal Train, which is used by both the Queen and other royals like Kate Middleton and Prince William when traveling closer to home. But what exactly is the Royal Train? In short, it’s a train specifically used to transport senior members of the Royal Family around Great Britain. (Think Air Force One, but a train—and British.) The current lineup of Royal Train carriages was formed in 1977 - the year of Queen Elizabeth II's Silver Jubilee—but the train was first used by Queen Victoria in June 1842. The 178 years since Queen Victoria's first train journey are filled with family trips, corgis, and some serious redecorating. Take a look at the Royal Train's storied history, in photos. On June 13, 1842, Queen Victoria became the first British monarch to travel by train. The trip was a 30-minute journey from Slough in Berkshire to Paddington Station in London. The Queen reportedly wrote in her diary that the trip was “delightful and so quick.” In 1869, she commissioned a pair of lavish coaches, including a saloon decked out in blue silk and 23-carat gold details. (The saloon was withdrawn in the early 1900s, and currently sits in the National Railway Museum in York.)
